title: Salmorejo
subtitle: Cordoban chilled tomato and bread cream, thicker than gazpacho.
excerpt: A Cordoban summer staple, salmorejo is a chilled tomato puree thickened with bread and emulsified with olive oil into a salmon-coloured cream. Garnished with chopped boiled egg and serrano ham. Twenty minutes of work, an hour in the fridge, four ample bowls.
sourceNotes:
Andalusian summer cookery, anchored in Cordoba, where salmorejo is a daily warm-weather staple. Documented in twentieth-century Spanish home cookbooks alongside gazpacho and ajoblanco, the trio of Andalusian chilled soups.

Salmorejo differs from gazpacho in two ways. It uses far more bread (the consistency is a cream, not a soup) and contains no peppers, cucumber, or vinegar. The base is tomato, bread, garlic, oil, and salt.

The colour is the marker. A proper salmorejo is pale salmon-pink from the emulsion of tomato and olive oil. Anything closer to red has not been blended long enough.

content.text: Salmorejo is the older Cordoban cousin of gazpacho. Tomato, bread, oil, garlic, salt. No peppers, no cucumber, no vinegar. The bread does the work; salmorejo wants the texture of double cream, not a soup, and the colour is a pale salmon pink rather than the deep red of gazpacho.

The dish is built on ripe summer tomatoes; out of season it does not work. Use the heaviest, ripest tomatoes you can buy, even the slightly bruised ones the greengrocer wants to move. A stale country loaf rather than fresh; the cell structure absorbs more tomato water and emulsifies better. A peppery extra-virgin olive oil; salmorejo without good oil has no body.
It is also called
salmorejo cordobés
. The garnish is set in stone. Scatter chopped boiled egg and a small mound of diced serrano ham over the top.
What you need

items.name: Bread
items.prepNote: stale country loaf, crusts off, torn into pieces

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: peeled, halved and the germ removed

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: peppery Spanish, plus extra for serving

items.name: Sherry vinegar
items.prepNote: Jerez vinegar if you can find it

content.text: You also need a high-powered blender. The texture is what separates salmorejo from a basic chilled tomato soup, and a stick blender will struggle to break the bread down to the right cream.
Method
Boil the eggs first

Drop {{eggs}} eggs into a small pan of simmering water. Cook for nine minutes for a firm yolk that holds its shape when chopped. Lift into cold water, peel when cool, set aside. You can do this a day ahead.
Soak the bread in the tomato

Drop {{tomato}} of tomato into the blender jug with the {{garlic}} of garlic. Pulse two or three times to break the tomatoes down. Add {{bread}} of torn bread, push it under the tomato water, and leave it to soak for twenty minutes. The bread softens completely; the blend that follows is far smoother for it.
Blend long, emulsify slow

Blend on the highest setting for a full minute. The mixture turns from chunky red to pale orange. With the motor still running, drizzle in {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil in a thin steady stream. The colour pales further to salmon pink and the texture thickens to a cream as the oil emulsifies. Add the {{sherry-vinegar}} of sherry vinegar if using and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt. Taste, adjust salt. The salt should be enough to make the tomato sing.
Pass through a sieve, optional

For a silkier finish, pass the cream through a fine sieve, pushing it through with the back of a ladle. You will lose the tomato skin and seed fragments. Most Cordobans do this; many home cooks skip it. Pick the texture you want.
Chill, then garnish

Cover and chill for at least an hour. Two is better. Ladle into shallow bowls. Chop the eggs to a fine dice and finely dice {{serrano-ham}} of serrano ham. Scatter the egg first, then the ham. Finish with a slow zigzag of olive oil. Serve cold.

attrs.intro: Salmorejo wants ripe fruit and a long blend. Most failures trace to one of the two.

items.fix: Run the blender for another minute with the oil drizzling in. The emulsion pales the colour. If it still reads red, the oil ratio is too low; another spoonful or two of olive oil corrects it.
items.cause: Not blended long enough or not enough oil.

items.fix: A longer soak helps. Pass the finished cream through a fine sieve, pressing with the back of a ladle, to take out skins and seeds.
items.cause: Bread not soaked long enough, or tomato skins not blended down.

items.fix: Tomatoes that taste like nothing raw will taste like nothing in the bowl. Look for soft, heavy fruit. Salt brings the flavour out; do not undersalt.
Drizzle the oil in a thin steady stream while the blender runs. A stick blender can struggle. Re-emulsify by blasting it again on high for fifteen seconds.
items.cause: Olive oil added too fast or blender not powerful enough to emulsify.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations

For a vegetarian salmorejo, skip the ham and add a finer dice of red pepper or a few black olives in its place. For a vegan version, drop the egg garnish too and replace with charred almond and a heavier drizzle of oil.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Salmorejo keeps two days in the fridge in a sealed jug, and is arguably better on day two as the flavours settle. Whisk briefly before serving to bring back the emulsion. It does not freeze; the texture breaks.
Leftovers find use as a dressing on grilled bread or as a sauce under poached fish. The cream holds its character when warmed gently, though salmorejo is meant to be eaten cold.
Where this dish lives

Salmorejo is one of three classic Andalusian chilled soups, along with gazpacho and ajoblanco. Cordoba claims it as a local invention, though older bread-and-tomato soups across Spain probably share roots. The dish is for the hottest months, when blending raw tomatoes with bread and serving it cold is the most sensible way to eat lunch.

The Cordoban version with egg and ham is the canonical garnish. Variations exist; some restaurants serve it without ham as a starter for vegetarian menus, and Andalusian home cooks adapt the proportions to whatever the tomato season offers. The structural choices are what matter: a lot of bread, no other vegetables, a long emulsifying blend.
